title: Smart biomass sampling protocol
author: Ferrarini, Andrea
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This deliverable presents a series of operational recommendations supported by a robust data-driven analytical workflow aimed at optimizing biomass sampling strategies for use in MRV context. These recommendations are structured within an Operational Recommendation Workflow (ORW), initially developed for cover crops but adaptable to a wide range of arable crops. The ORW supports the design and validation of future sampling campaigns that require ground measurements to calibrate and validate Above Ground Dry Biomass (AGDB) or yield estimates produced by crop and ecosystem models embedded in Operational Processing Chains (OPCs) used to quantify carbon removals in CF programs. Specifically, the ORW helps identify the Minimum Sample Size (MSS) required either to (i) train an accurate and robust spatial Random Forest (RF) model for AGDB estimation (MSS type 1), which can then support the validation of the sampling points selected by smart biomass sampling strategy, or to (ii) calibrate and validate AGDB/yield predictions from existing mechanistic models (MSS type 2) to meet the accuracy thresholds required by certification bodies.  
  The  target audience  for this deliverable includes MRV professionals involved in biomass sampling campaigns, as well as modelers tasked with calibrating and validating crop and ecosystem models integrated into OPCs under specific CF programs. These users share the need for practical, scalable, and cost-effective sampling strategies that ensure model accuracy and compliance with certification protocols.  
  The  main activity  involved developing and testing an ORW to compare 11 geospatial smart biomass sampling strategies for future use in crop biomass sampling. The ORW was structured into four sequential phases:  Phase 1  involved the creation of an initial dataset combining measured AGDB and remote sensing predictors (if not already available).  Phase 2  focused on training and validating a spatial RF model to estimate AGDB or yield. The output of this model served as a virtual representation of the full population, enabling the comparison of the sampling strategies.  Phase 3  consisted of simulating the 11 different sampling strategies using the virtual full-population. Finally,  Phase 4  introduced a structured, multi-objective evaluation framework to assess the performance of each strategy. This evaluation included three tiers: Tier 1 assessed each strategy’s ability to select the MSS (type 1) for accurate model training; Tier 2 examined how well each strategy could identify a MSS (type 2) that is representative of the full distribution of the target variable (AGDB or yield); and Tier 3 tested the generalizability of MSS type 2 recommendations across countries using key AGDB predictors. This tiered, holistic assessment enabled the identification of the most effective and scalable sampling strategies for operational use in carbon farming programs.  
  The  main outcome  of this deliverable is a series of operational recommendations to guide MRV professionals and model developers in designing efficient biomass or yield sampling strategies and a step-by-step guide on how to operationally use the ORW in OPC development context. These recommendations are especially relevant in the context of CF programs where reliable ground crop data are essential to guarantee that any crop/ecosystem model with or without RS data assimilation is continuously calibrated and validated within an OPC.   
   Result 1    cLHS-based sampling strategies were identified as the most effective for collecting an initial sampling dataset and building a robust spatial RF model (lowest MSS type 1 and highest model performance). cLHS also proved to be optimal in identifying MSS required to capture the full variability of AGDB, ensuring accurate OPC model calibration and validation. Public code and reproducible methods support the application of these strategies.  
   Result 2    Through a multi-tier comparison, two types of MSS were defined. For training robustly a spatial RF model (MSS type 1), an optimal range of 90–100 sampling units (ESUs) was identified. Including a separate validation set (30–40 ESUs), the full sampling requirement increases to approximately 120–130 samples. A similar MSS range also applies for MSS type 2 (around 90-100 samples) when biomass samples must be taken using the best sampling strategy identified to calibrate and validate crop/ecosystem models. This MSS is the one identified with cLHS-based sampling strategies and achieves the accuracy threshold required by certification bodies (95% confidence interval). This is especially important for cover crops, known for their high spatial and temporal variability in biomass production.  
   Results 3    Informed cLHS and Hybrid cLHS emerged as the most transferable sampling strategies across diverse biophysical variables and pedoclimatic conditions. These approaches leverage auxiliary data or cluster-based structures to better reflect environmental heterogeneity, making them suitable for broader application in varying geographical and agronomic contexts.   
  The findings of this work have direct  implications  for both MRV practitioners and model developers engaged in CF initiatives and OPC developments. By identifying the most effective sampling strategies and optimal sample sizes, the ORW offers practical guidance for designing biomass sampling campaigns that are cost-efficient, scientifically robust, and adaptable across different crops and regions. This supports more accurate calibration and validation of mechanistic models used in OPCs to quantify carbon removals. In particular, the transferability shown by informed and hybrid cLHS smart sampling strategies across pedoclimatic contexts makes them valuable tools for large-scale, multi-region CF programs aiming to implement consistent and reliable crop monitoring protocols.     From a  research perspective , the work lays the foundation for further innovation in biomass prediction and sampling design. Future efforts can focus on adaptive and dynamic sampling approaches based on active learning. In this approach, an initial model is used not only to predict biomass but also to generate spatial uncertainty maps that highlight where predictions are less reliable. Sampling efforts can then be directed to these high-uncertainty areas, making data collection more targeted and efficient. Over time, this strategy could enhance the validation of existing crop models—or even replace them—when such spatial Rs-driven ML models offer superior performance in estimating crop biomass/yield than mechanistic models.  
  This deliverable contributes to the Soil Mission objectives by improving the methodological framework for designing cost-effective and scalable biomass sampling strategies to support the quantification of CF program impacts. It directly aligns with the needs of the EU Carbon Removal and Carbon Farming Certification Framework (CRCF) by offering guidance on the MSS and smart sampling strategies needed to calibrate and validate crop and ecosystem models used in MRV systems. The findings are particularly relevant for CF project developers and modelers aiming to develop or refine soil and biomass monitoring schemes that meet CRCF requirements. By promoting harmonized, science-based approaches to field data collection and model validation, this work supports cross-country comparability and strengthens the integrity of CF certification systems.  
  To  conclude , this deliverable provides a structured and replicable analytical workflow to guide the design of efficient biomass sampling strategies for CF applications. By identifying optimal sample sizes and robust, generalizable sampling approaches, it offers practical tools for improving the accuracy of model calibration and validation within MRV systems. These findings strengthen the methodological foundation of the project and contribute to the development of transparent, scalable, and cost-effective carbon accounting schemes. While the ORW was developed and tested within this deliverable for sampling strategy optimization, it is also intended for future operational use: depending on the availability of input data and models, certain steps can be skipped. A step-by-step guideline (ORW for future use) supports MRV users in adapting the workflow to different operational scenarios, helping them determine either the minimum number of samples needed or the accuracy achievable with a fixed sampling effort. Future work will build on these results by integrating adaptive, uncertainty-driven sampling methods to further enhance prediction accuracy and operational relevance across diverse agricultural contexts.
